english: marians, from an excessive regard of the Ssk. language and in fond imitation of its peculiarities, have also adopted seven in number. — rnam-(par) smin(-pa) (rnam (par) smin (pa)) retaliation, requital, of good or evil deeds, committed in former lives, of good actions by prosperity (las-ₒpró (las 'phro)), of bad ones by misery and sufferings (lan-čágs (lan chags)), very frq.; sdig-pai rnam-par smin-pa myóṅ-ba (sdig pa'i rnam par smin pa myong ba) Dzl. — rnam-(par) bżág(-pa) (rnam (par) bzhag (pa)) 1. to distinguish, to put in order, arrange, classify Wdn., Thgy., ..... sgó-nas (sgo nas) according to ... (certain points or facts). 2. to consider a person or thing as fully equal or equivalent to another, to substitute one for the other, C.; rnam-bżag (rnam bzhag) sbst., Lex. व्यवस्था 1. placing apart, separating; distinction. 2. arrangement, position, = gnás-lugs (gnas lugs) 1. — rnam-(par) rig(-pa) (rnam (par) rig (pa)) and śés(-pa) (shes (pa)), as a vb., 1. to know fully, to understand thoroughly. 2. rnám-par śés-pai lús-càn-rnams (rnam par shes pa'i lus can rnams) Dom. rational, or at least animated, beings, opp. to inanimate nature; as a sbst., gen. rnam-śès (rnam shes), विज्ञान: 1. etymologically: perfect knowledge, consciousness, Köpp. I, 604. 2. in philosophy: one of the five púṅ-po (phung po), perceptions, cognitions, Was. (of which there are six, if the knowledge acquired by the inner sense is included) also in Mil. frq., e. g. sgo lṅai rnam-śes (sgo lnga'i rnam shes) (cf. sgo gsum (sgo gsum)). 3. in pop. language: soul, e. g. of the departed, (later literature and col.) (The significations 2 and 3, I presume, should be distinguished, as is done here, according to the different spheres in which they are used and not be explained one out of the other, as is attempted Burn. I, 503. Schr. gives here, as in most cases, the signification used in col. language.) 4. rnam-rig (rnam rig) Was. (307) idea, notion; Tar. often = न्याय, also विज्ञ, rnam-rig-tu bkrál-pa (rnam rig tu bkral pa) 'explained in the sense of the idealists', Schf.; rnam-rig daṅ rtóg-gei bstan-bcòs (rnam rig dang rtog ge'i bstan bcos) logical and dialectical Shastras. — rnam-bśád (rnam bshad) explanation Tar.

rnams, in B. the usual sign of the plural, in col. language little used, esp. in W., meaning, acc. to its etymology, piece by piece; hence its use is not a strict grammatical rule, but more or less arbitrary; it is mostly omitted, when the plural is otherwise indicated, e. g. after definite and indefinite numerals; it may be used, however, not only in these instances (ₒkór máṅ-po-rnams ('khor mang po rnams) many servants), but also after collective nouns (dge-ₒdún-rnams (dge 'dun rnams)), at the end of enumerations (= de tams-cád (de thams cad)), after general expressions, such as: gaṅ yód(-pa)-rnams (gang yod (pa) rnams) whatever they were, after other plural-signs (... dag-rnams (... dag rnams) etc.). Cf. rnám-pa (rnam pa) 2.
rnar, for rná-bar (rna bar), q. v.

I. 1. rest Cs., lus rnál-du gnás-par gyúr (lus rnal du gnas par gyur) - to his body obtained rest Tar.; esp. tranquillity of mind, composedness, absence of passion, sems rnál-du mi gnás-par (sems rnal du mi gnas par) his soul having no rest Tar.; rnál-du ₒdúg-pa (rnal du 'dug pa), or ₒkód-pa ('khod pa), Mil.: rnál-mar sdód-pa (rnal mar sdod pa) id.; rig-pa rnál-du ₒbébs-pa (rig pa rnal du 'bebs pa) to give one's mind up to perfect rest Thgr.; rnal-ₒbyór (rnal 'byor) 1. योग, meditation, nearly the same as tiṅ-ṅe-ₒdzin (ting nge 'dzin) and bsam-gtán (bsam gtan) Mil., but chiefly when it is considered as the business of life; rnal-ₒbyór-rgyúd (rnal 'byor rgyud), योगतन्त्र, Tar. frq. 2. often for rnal-ₒbyór-pa (rnal 'byor pa). — rnal-ₒbyór-pa (rnal 'byor pa) योगिन्, योगाचार्य, devotee, saint, sage, miracle-worker frq. — 2. Sch. also: personal, visible, essential (?) — Tar. 201, 6. 22: bstán-pa rnál-ma (bstan pa rnal ma)? — II. often for mnal (mnal).

B., རྣོན་པོ་ rnón-po (rnon po) usual form, 1. sharp, acute, edged, pointed; rno-méd (rno med) C. dull, blunt; rno ṗyúṅ-ba (rno phyung ba) to sharpen, grind, whet Sch. (like k'a ₒdón-pa (kha 'don pa)); rno lén-pa (rno len pa) to get sharp, to be sharpened; rno-ṗyuṅ (rno phyung) name of males. — 2. this word is applied by the Tibetans to the chemical qualities of things, though not quite in the same way as we do, as they ascribe a 'sharp' taste to the flesh of beasts of prey, to the bile etc. Med. — 3. rig-pa rnó-ba (rig pa rno ba) sharp, clever, shrewd, Glr., blo rnó-ba (blo rno ba) talented, gifted, dbáṅ-po rnó-ba (dbang po rno ba) acute, sagacious.
